title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana data — the consistent memory climb pattern is really helpful.

Could you share some environment details so we can narrow this down?

- What **TaskFlow version** are you running?
- What **OS and runtime** is the server on (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04, Node 18, Docker container)?
- What **database** are you using (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite), and is it on the same host or separate?
- Any **reverse proxy** in front of TaskFlow (e.g., Nginx, Apache)?

This will help us figure out where the memory leak is likely occurring.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We're running TaskFlow v2.3 — we upgraded from v2.2 about a week ago, which is actually right around when this problem started. The server is on Ubuntu 22.04, it's an 8GB RAM VM. I'm not sure what runtime you mean exactly, we just installed it following the official docs. Database is PostgreSQL on a separate host. No reverse proxy, we're accessing it directly for now (it's an internal tool). Come to think of it, the timing with the v2.3 upgrade is pretty suspicious — we didn't have this issue on v2.2.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Great catch on the timing — the v2.3 upgrade coinciding with the start of this issue is a strong clue.

One more thing that would really help narrow this down: are there any **error messages or warnings in the TaskFlow server logs**, especially as the slowdown progresses? For example, out-of-memory warnings, connection pool exhaustion, or repeated errors that increase over time.

If you're not sure where the logs are, they're typically in `/var/log/taskflow/` or visible via `journalctl -u taskflow` depending on how it was installed.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I checked the logs with journalctl and I don't see any explicit out-of-memory errors or crashes — the server doesn't actually crash, it just gets really slow. I did notice there are a LOT of log entries related to the notification system, like it's registering push notification handlers over and over. The volume of those log lines seems to grow throughout the day. I don't see any connection pool warnings or database errors though. The database server itself seems fine — CPU and memory on that host are stable. It really does seem like something on the TaskFlow server side is accumulating and not getting cleaned up.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in TaskFlow v2.3: notification handlers appear to accumulate, causing progressive slowdown","problem":"After upgrading from v2.2 to v2.3, the TaskFlow server's memory usage climbs steadily from ~500MB to 4GB+ over the course of a day, causing page loads to exceed 10 seconds and API timeouts. The server requires a daily restart to recover. The issue did not occur on v2.2.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 release likely introduced a bug in the notification system where push notification handlers are registered repeatedly without being cleaned up. The growing volume of notification-related log entries throughout the day supports this — event listeners or handlers are accumulating and consuming memory.","reproduction_steps":["Install or upgrade to TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with ~8GB RAM","Connect a PostgreSQL database and configure normally per official docs","Allow ~200 users to use the system through a normal workday","Monitor memory usage over several hours — expect steady climb from ~500MB","Observe increasing notification handler log entries via journalctl -u taskflow","By end of day, memory reaches 4GB+ and response times degrade to 10+ seconds"],"environment":"Ubuntu 22.04, 8GB RAM VM, TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2), PostgreSQL on separate host, no reverse proxy, ~200 active users","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 users on this instance experience progressively degrading performance daily, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Requires daily manual restart as a workaround.","recommended_fix":"Diff the notification system code between v2.2 and v2.3. Look for event listener or handler registrations that run on each request or on a recurring basis without corresponding cleanup/deregistration. Likely candidates: a subscribe/addEventListener call that was moved into a per-request code path, or a removed cleanup/dispose call. Add proper handler deregistration or deduplicate handler registration so each handler is registered only once.","proposed_test_case":"Write a test that simulates repeated notification registration cycles (e.g., 1000 iterations of whatever triggers handler setup) and asserts that the number of registered handlers and memory usage remain bounded. Additionally, a soak/load test running for several simulated hours with concurrent users should show stable memory.","information_gaps":["Exact Node.js/runtime version (reporter was unsure; docs-based install likely uses a known version)","Whether the issue reproduces with fewer users or requires the full 200-user load","Specific v2.3 changelog entries related to the notification system"]}
